questions.question: Explain the steps involved in opening and reading a dictionary file in Unix System Programming. What error checking is essential?
keyPoints: Use fopen() with "r" mode to open the dictionary file
Check if fopen() returns NULL to detect file open failures
Use fgets() to read lines (words) from the file
Remove trailing newline characters using strcspn() or similar
Process each word as required by the application
Use fclose() to close the file when done
Always check return values for error handling

questions.question: What is the standard format of the Unix 'words' dictionary file? List two practical applications that use this dictionary.
keyPoints: Plain text file with one word per line
Words are typically sorted alphabetically
No definitions or metadata - just raw words
Used by spell checkers (spell, aspell)
Used for password security testing (dictionary attacks)
Used in word games and text processing utilities

questions.question: Write a C code snippet to count the number of words in the system dictionary file. Include proper error handling.
keyPoints: Open file with fopen("/usr/share/dict/words", "r")
Check for NULL return on fopen()
Initialize counter to zero
Use fgets() in a loop until NULL is returned
Increment counter for each successful read
Print the total count
Close the file with fclose()

questions.question: Why is it important to remove the newline character after reading a word with fgets()? How is this accomplished in C?
keyPoints: Newline character is included in the buffer by fgets() if the line fits
The newline can cause incorrect string comparisons
Using strcmp() would fail if comparing "apple\n" with "apple"
The newline can cause incorrect output formatting
Use strcspn(buffer, "\n") to find newline position
Replace newline with null terminator: buffer[strcspn(buffer, "\n")] = '\0'

questions.question: Compare linear search versus hash table approach for dictionary word lookup. When would you choose each method?
keyPoints: Linear search: O(n) time, no memory overhead for structure
Hash table: O(1) average time, requires memory for table structure
Choose linear search for: small dictionaries, one-time lookups, memory-constrained systems
Choose hash table for: large dictionaries, frequent lookups, real-time applications
Trade-off between memory usage and speed

questions.question: What security implications are associated with dictionary files in Unix systems?
keyPoints: Dictionary files are used in dictionary attacks for password cracking
Weak passwords found in dictionaries are easily guessed
System administrators should ensure dictionary files have appropriate permissions
Applications should implement account lockout policies
Consider using salted hashes to prevent rainbow table attacks
Users should avoid using dictionary words as passwords

questions.question: Write a complete C program that reads the Unix dictionary file and checks whether a given word (passed as a command-line argument) exists in the dictionary. The program should handle all error conditions and display appropriate messages.
keyPoints: Include necessary headers: stdio.h, stdlib.h, string.h
Check command-line argument count (argc should be 2)
Open dictionary file /usr/share/dict/words or /usr/dict/words
Handle file open failure with perror()
Read words using fgets() in a loop
Remove newline character from each word read
Use strcmp() to compare input word with dictionary words
Consider case sensitivity - convert both to same case if needed
Print word found or not found message
Close file and return appropriate exit code
Handle EOF and error conditions

questions.question: Design and explain a spell-checking program that reads a text file and reports all words that are not found in the system dictionary. Your solution should include: file handling, dictionary loading strategy, word extraction, comparison logic, and output formatting.
keyPoints: Two-file approach: dictionary file + text file to check
Load dictionary into efficient data structure (hash table or binary search tree)
Open text file and read using fgets() or character-by-character parsing
Extract individual words (handle punctuation, case conversion)
For each word, check existence in dictionary structure
Report words not found in dictionary as potential misspellings
Consider performance: build dictionary once, check many words
Handle edge cases: hyphenated words, apostrophes, numbers
Proper memory management and file closure
Error handling for file operations

questions.question: Explain the concept of memory management when reading large dictionary files. Write a C program that demonstrates efficient memory usage by reading a large dictionary file in a streaming manner rather than loading it entirely into memory.
keyPoints: Large dictionaries (100K+ words) can consume significant memory if loaded entirely
Streaming approach: read line-by-line using fixed-size buffer
Buffer size like 256 or 512 bytes is sufficient for most words
fgets() automatically handles large files by reading in chunks
Process each word immediately after reading, don't accumulate
This approach uses O(1) memory regardless of dictionary size
Compare with loading entire file: O(n) memory for n-byte file
Demonstrate with code using char buffer[256] and fgets()
Explain when the streaming approach might not be suitable
Mention that for repeated lookups, building an index is better
